displayTitle: When the Source Survives but the Shielding Doesn't
kicker: RAD SAFETY // FIRE INVOLVING SEALED SOURCES

beats.narration: A sealed source is built to survive a fire. Its shielding is not. That single gap drives everything you do next.

payload.title: THE SOURCE SURVIVES. THE SHIELDING MELTS.
payload.sub: Fire rarely opens the capsule. It strips the lead around it.

beats.narration: Lead melts at six hundred twenty-one Fahrenheit. A real fire runs far hotter, yet the special-form capsule rides out eight hundred Celsius.

payload.heading: Why fire changes the problem
payload.note: LEAD SLUMPS EARLY · CAPSULE SURVIVES · SHIELDING GONE, DEVICE LOOKS NORMAL

beats.narration: So a device can leave the flames looking normal with its shielding gone. Your response follows one ranking: people, then the source, then property.

chips.s: Warn, evacuate, account for the crew
Crank in only if quick and safe
Equipment is replaceable, dose is not
No gear stops an unshielded gamma field

beats.narration: Call nine-one-one yourself and say radioactive material is involved. Then brief command: nuclide, activity, location, shielding status, your readings.

payload.heading: Make the call, own the briefing

tiles.s: Say a sealed source is involved
The fact that changes their tactics

beats.narration: And after the flames are out, nobody re-enters until a meter proves it safe. Shielding loss is invisible. Only the survey reopens that corner.

payload.sub: Lead can melt with no visible sign — appearance proves nothing about the shield.
